era_context: Late Bronze Age collapse in its mature phase. Egypt under Ramesses III continues to face external military pressure and internal economic strain. The Eastern Mediterranean remains disrupted by Sea Peoples and population movements. Neo-Hittite city-states maintain fragmented political authority in Anatolia and Syria. The Shang Dynasty in China persists but faces mounting pressure from Zhou peoples. Iron-working technology continues to spread across the Old World.

events.title: Ramesses III Year 10: Military consolidation and economic pressures
events.description: Ramesses III's tenth regnal year continues the pattern of military campaigns and administrative management. Egyptian sources indicate ongoing military expenditures and temple endowments, but also suggest increasing economic strain from maintaining military forces and managing the aftermath of Sea Peoples invasions. The pharaoh's ability to project power beyond Egypt's borders is diminishing, marking a transition toward the decline of New Kingdom authority.
events.certainty_note: Egyptian regnal year dating is secure. Absolute dating to 1132 BCE depends on 20th Dynasty chronology (±10–15 years).
cross_references: Part of Ramesses III's reign (c. -1186 to -1155 BCE)

events.title: Levantine coastal cities experience disruption and population displacement
events.description: Archaeological evidence from Levantine coastal sites (Sidon, Tyre, Byblos, Megiddo) indicates ongoing disruption, destruction layers, and population displacement during this period. Some cities are abandoned or severely depopulated; others are reoccupied by new populations. This reflects the broader Late Bronze Age collapse phenomenon, with causes likely including Sea Peoples raids, economic disruption, and environmental stress (drought, famine). The Levantine coast transitions from Egyptian-dominated Late Bronze Age political structures to Iron Age configurations.
key_figures: Local Levantine rulers (names largely unpreserved in contemporary sources)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Levantine excavations (Sidon, Tyre, Megiddo, etc.)
Egyptian administrative records mentioning Levantine cities

events.certainty_note: Archaeological destruction layers and settlement patterns are well-documented, but precise dating to individual years is difficult. Radiocarbon dating and stratigraphic analysis place major disruptions c. 1200–1100 BCE, with 1132 BCE falling within this range.
cross_references: Part of broader Late Bronze Age collapse c. -1200 to -1050

events.title: Iron-working technology spreads in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East
events.description: During the late 12th century BCE, iron-working technology becomes increasingly common in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East, though bronze remains the dominant metal for weapons and tools. Iron artifacts appear in archaeological contexts across Egypt, the Levant, Anatolia, and Cyprus. This technological transition is gradual and uneven, with iron initially used for prestige items and decorative purposes before becoming practical for tools and weapons. The spread of iron-working is associated with the Late Bronze Age collapse and the emergence of Iron Age societies.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from multiple sites (metallurgical analysis of artifacts)
Egyptian temple reliefs and inscriptions mentioning iron

events.certainty_note: The spread of iron-working technology is well-attested archaeologically. The process is gradual, and 1132 BCE falls within the period of increasing iron use, though bronze remains dominant.
cross_references: Part of broader technological transition from Bronze Age to Iron Age (c. -1200 to -1000 BCE)

events.title: Shang Dynasty oracle bone divinations continue (China)
events.description: Late Shang oracle bone inscriptions continue to record divinations on warfare, agriculture, and ritual. The practice reflects ongoing administrative and religious functions of the Shang court. While specific events for 1132 BCE cannot be isolated, the oracle bone corpus as a whole provides evidence of Shang institutional continuity during a period of increasing external pressure.
events.certainty_note: Oracle bones are contemporary artifacts, but precise year-by-year dating of Shang chronology is uncertain (±50–100 years possible).
cross_references: Part of late Shang Dynasty period (c. -1250 to -1046 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The 'Late Bronze Age collapse' is increasingly recognized by scholars as a complex, multi-causal phenomenon rather than a unified catastrophe. Some scholars emphasize climate change and drought; others stress internal economic and political instability; still others highlight the role of migration and warfare. The Sea Peoples may not have been a primary cause of collapse but rather a symptom of broader disruption. Additionally, the transition from bronze to iron was gradual and uneven; iron did not immediately replace bronze, and the 'Iron Age' is a modern periodization that ancient actors would not have recognized.
historiographic_note: Egyptian sources continue to dominate the historical record, creating a Mediterranean-centric perspective. Levantine and Anatolian sources are largely lost or fragmentary. Chinese sources are limited to oracle bones and much later compilations. The archaeological record provides crucial evidence but requires careful interpretation to avoid imposing modern categories (e.g., 'collapse,' 'migration,' 'technological revolution') onto ancient processes that were experienced differently by contemporaries. The 'Late Bronze Age collapse' framework, while useful for organizing evidence, may obscure regional variation and the agency of local actors.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses III's military campaigns and administrative challenges continue across his reign
Levantine disruption is part of broader Late Bronze Age collapse phenomenon
Iron-working technology spreads gradually, contributing to Iron Age emergence
